By. André Orban. -. 16 July 2026. 0. 5.Read more
Home Airlines United Airlines FAA proposes $255,000 fine against United Airlines over alleged drug-testing violations
Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) has proposed a civil penalty of $255,000 against United Airlines for allegedly violating federal drug and alcohol testing regulations.
United Airlines has 30 days from receipt of the FAA’s enforcement letter to respond to the proposed civil penalty."},{"url":"https://www.eckertseamans.com/legal-updates/aviation-regulatory-update-april-2026","title":"Aviation Regulatory Update - April 2026 | Eckert Seamans","content":"On April 16, 2026, the Federal Aviation Administration finalized scheduling limits at Chicago O’Hare International Airport to mitigate congestion and prevent widespread flight delays.
Failure to comply with scheduling limits could result in civil penalties of up to $75,000 per flight above the prescribed limits.
On April 17, 2026, DOT’s Office of General Counsel published Order 2026-4-11 which amended a prior consent order issued against Frontier Airlines.
A $650,000 civil penalty was assessed against Frontier even after the U.S. air carrier complied with the investigation conducted by DOT’s Office of Aviation Consumer Protection and settled the matter to avoid protracted litigation.
A similar $304,272 civil penalty was proposed this month against Southwest Airlines for allegedly failing to perform follow-up drug or alcohol testing for 11 Southwest employees between August 2021 and July 2024.
In addition to Spring City and Southwest, the FAA also proposed a $255,000 civil penalty against American Airlines over allegations that it permitted several flight attendants who previously tested positive on drug and alcohol tests to resume their safety-sensitive duties before all required follow-up testing was completed.
On March 31, 2026, the United States Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit issued an opinion in which a three-judge panel declined to reopen the criminal case against Boeing for conspiring to defraud regulators in connection with the FAA’s evaluation of Boeing’s 737 MAX aircraft."},{"url":"https://www.faa.gov/","title":"Federal Aviation Administration","content":"The FAA will allow Boeing to resume issuing airworthiness certificates at the end of the production process for all 737 MAX and 787 airplanes."},{"url":"https://www.faa.gov/newsroom/faa-proposes-255000-fine-against-united-airlines-alleged-drug-and-alcohol-violation","title":"FAA Proposes $255000 Fine Against United Airlines for ...","date":"1 day ago","content":"1 day ago — FAA Proposes $255,000 Fine Against United Airlines for Alleged Drug and Alcohol Violation. The agency alleges the employee had not been evaluated by a substance abuse professional, had not produced a verified negative return-to-duty drug test and was not enrolled in the mandatory follow-up testing programme. The FAA said the flight attendant had previously been dismissed by United in 2021 for refusing to undergo a drug test before later being rehired.
